title: Markdown Reader
summary: A fast, distraction-free desktop app for reading and previewing Markdown files.
highlights: Instant live preview with synced scrolling between source and rendered view
Keyboard-first navigation and a command palette for zero-mouse reading
Handles large documents without lag by rendering only the visible viewport
content:
## The problem

I read a lot of Markdown — notes, docs, READMEs — and wanted a native, snappy
reader that stayed out of the way instead of a heavyweight editor.

## The approach

A small desktop shell wraps a rendering pipeline that streams the parsed document
into a virtualized view, so only what's on screen is laid out. Scroll position is
kept in sync between the raw source and the rendered output.

## Where it stands

Complete and in daily use. It's the kind of tool I built for myself first and
then cleaned up enough to share.

title: Mini DBMS
summary: A from-scratch storage engine: slotted pages, a B+ tree index, and write-ahead logging.
highlights: Slotted page format that packs variable-length records without wasted space
B+ tree index with correct leaf vs internal node split rules
Write-ahead log so an interrupted write never corrupts a page
content:
## The problem

I wanted to understand how a database actually puts bytes on disk and finds them
again — not at the SQL layer, but underneath it, at the page and index level.

## The approach

I started at the storage layer: a fixed-size page is the unit of I/O, and a slotted
directory inside each page tracks where every record lives. On top of that sits a B+
tree for ordered lookups, and a write-ahead log for durability.

## Where it stands

The storage engine and B+ tree inserts work; the WAL and crash recovery are in
progress. The build logs live in the **Building a Mini DBMS** blog series.

title: RAFT + Key-Value Store
summary: A replicated key-value store built on a hand-rolled Raft consensus implementation.
highlights: Leader election and log replication implemented from the Raft paper
A linearizable key-value store layered on top of the replicated log
Fault-injection tests that kill and partition nodes mid-write
content:
## The problem

Distributed consensus is the part of systems work I understand least by reading and
most by building. Raft is designed to be understandable, which makes it the right
place to start.

## The approach

The plan is to implement Raft in stages — leader election first, then log
replication, then snapshotting — and only then layer a key-value store on top so the
consensus core stays testable in isolation.

## Where it stands

Planned. Design notes and the build series will land before the first commit.

title: Sim Racing Telemetry Tool
summary: A live telemetry overlay that reads sim racing data and coaches you with real-time tips.
highlights: Parses the game's live telemetry stream over UDP at 60 Hz without dropping frames
Real-time coaching tips: braking points, throttle trace, and cornering lines
Lap comparison overlay so you can see exactly where a faster lap gained time
content:
## The problem

Sim racing produces a firehose of telemetry, but the raw numbers don't tell you
*how* to go faster. I wanted something that turned the live stream into actionable
coaching while I'm still on track.

## The approach

The tool listens to the game's telemetry feed, buffers laps, and compares your
current lap against your best. A lightweight overlay surfaces the few tips that
actually matter in the moment — brake later here, get on throttle earlier there.

## Where it stands

In progress. Live parsing and the throttle/brake traces work; the coaching
heuristics and lap-delta overlay are being tuned against real driving data.
